text: The first thing that comes to mind is that each platform requires a different optimizer, but the optimization logic can be shared in some places.

elements.text: The first thing that comes to mind is that each platform requires a different optimizer, but the optimization logic can be shared in some places.

text:
what do you mean by binary optimization?
 most optimizations are done in previous phases in higher level representations like MLIR, SSA etc

elements.text:
what do you mean by binary optimization?
 most optimizations are done in previous phases in higher level representations like MLIR, SSA etc

text: I'm interested in a tool common to all languages.

elements.text: I'm interested in a tool common to all languages.

text: binary is not common because you can have binaries for different architectures, at that level you lost that context

elements.text: binary is not common because you can have binaries for different architectures, at that level you lost that context

text: the lowest level you can optimize something is at the instruction level for each architecture, which is not common to all targets

elements.text: the lowest level you can optimize something is at the instruction level for each architecture, which is not common to all targets

text: that's why llvm exists, it's a low level instruction representation that can generate instructions for multiple architectures, everyone can contribute optimizations at that level and every language above and every architecture below benefits

elements.text: that's why llvm exists, it's a low level instruction representation that can generate instructions for multiple architectures, everyone can contribute optimizations at that level and every language above and every architecture below benefits

text: This is initially clear. What specifically for x86-64?

elements.text: This is initially clear. What specifically for x86-64?

text: which one, that's a family of processors all with different capabilities and subset of supported instructions :slightly_smiling_face:

elements.text: which one, that's a family of processors all with different capabilities and subset of supported instructions

text: Right. Although, as I have noticed, optimizers aimed at optimizing intermediate code turn out to be worse for a particular architecture.

elements.text: Right. Although, as I have noticed, optimizers aimed at optimizing intermediate code turn out to be worse for a particular architecture.

text: yes, but optimizers for a particular architecture don't work for all the others :smile:

elements.text: yes, but optimizers for a particular architecture don't work for all the others

text: you can solve that with a JIT

elements.text: you can solve that with a JIT

text: the JIT knows in which particular processor is running and can emit the best instructions for that processor

elements.text: the JIT knows in which particular processor is running and can emit the best instructions for that processor

text: CPUs also do optimization at the instruction level to some extent. I think the reason that you see optimization happening at higher levels is that the levels are more expressive and easier to reason about abstractly which becomes harder after that information is lost.

elements.text: CPUs also do optimization at the instruction level to some extent. I think the reason that you see optimization happening at higher levels is that the levels are more expressive and easier to reason about abstractly which becomes harder after that information is lost.

text: Jonathan, who was the audience for this particular video back in 2005? I imagine you presented in person back then?

elements.text: Jonathan, who was the audience for this particular video back in 2005? I imagine you presented in person back then?

text: In 2005 YouTube had just started up. Jon Udell had just written his blog post coining 'screencast' in 2004.

elements.text: In 2005 YouTube had just started up. Jon Udell had just written his blog post coining 'screencast' in 2004.

text: I must have done this screencast as practice for my Onward presentation and as content for my blog. I miss blogging - it was so much more earnest and innocent than our social media hellscape. I keep saying this Slack should be regressed to a 90's BBS.

elements.text: I must have done this screencast as practice for my Onward presentation and as content for my blog. I miss blogging - it was so much more earnest and innocent than our social media hellscape. I keep saying this Slack should be regressed to a 90's BBS.

text:
The dream is alive. In 2024 I went back over 5 years of posts on Mastodon and this Slack and a few other places and started hosting a devlog for myself on my own site. It's helpful to see just the stuff I originate, without replies to others or boosts of others (all great things, but for a different context).

I'm not quite all the way at <https://indieweb.org/POSSE|POSSE>, but rather <https://indieweb.org/PESOS|PESOS>.
